Q1: Honestly, my study routine was all over the place this semester. I'd go days without touching anything, then suddenly pull these marathon sessions when I realized deadlines were coming up. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time making diagrams and color-coded notes, but the actual scheduling was chaotic. With six online courses, I was constantly putting out fires rather than having any real system. I'd usually study late at night when my anxiety would kick in about falling behind.
Q2: My pattern was completely deadline-driven, no question about it. Some weeks I'd barely look at course material, then other weeks I'd be studying 22 hours straight across a few days. When I had extracurricular commitments or when multiple assignments lined up, everything got pushed to the last possible moment. I wish I could say I had regular study times, but that wasn't my reality this semester.
Q3: Usually panic, if I'm being honest. I'd get an email notification about a deadline or suddenly remember I hadn't checked something in days. Sometimes a classmate from my peer support group would message me asking about an assignment, and that would remind me to log in. Occasionally I'd log in just to watch some lecture videos when I was feeling motivated, but those moments were rare.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became routine for me—it was more like this thing I dreaded opening because I knew I'd see how behind I was. There were maybe a couple of weeks mid-semester where I tried to check in daily, but that fell apart quickly when other life stuff got in the way. My attendance being so low reflects that—I just couldn't make it a habit.
Q5: The video lectures were genuinely helpful because I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the visual content. I spent over 200 minutes watching videos because that's how I learn best—seeing concepts illustrated rather than just reading about them. The peer interaction features were also useful when I actually used them, because talking things through with classmates helped cement ideas in my mind.
Q6: The discussion forums were basically useless to me—I only posted twice all semester. Reading through long text threads just doesn't work for my brain; I need visuals. I also ignored a lot of the supplementary readings because when you're already struggling to keep up with six courses, optional text-heavy resources are the first thing to go. It felt like survival mode most of the time.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or if I could see immediate practical application. Visual content like diagrams, video explanations, or interactive elements grabbed my attention. If something felt like busywork or wasn't clearly going to help my grade, I'd skip it. Time is precious when you're juggling this much.
Q8: Required activities got done—eventually, even if last minute. Optional activities basically didn't exist in my world this semester. There's something about knowing something is required that forces you to push through even when you're exhausted or anxious. Optional stuff felt like a luxury I couldn't afford when I was barely keeping my head above water with the mandatory work.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded lecture slides and videos to study on my tablet offline, made handwritten visual notes and mind maps, and used YouTube for alternative explanations of difficult concepts. A lot of my actual learning happened with materials spread out on my desk, drawing connections between ideas in ways that wouldn't show up anywhere in the LMS. My assignment scores being high reflects this offline work.
Q10: There were entire weekends where I'd be studying intensely from printed materials and hand-drawn concept maps, but my LMS would show zero activity. I'd screenshot important slides and annotate them on my tablet. Sometimes I'd have study sessions with peers from my support group at the library, working through problems together with no digital footprint. The LMS only sees when I'm logged in, not when I'm actually learning.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture video once, then watch it three or four more times offline while taking notes. Same with PDFs—I'd access them once to download, then spend hours reading and annotating the printed version. My actual engagement with materials was way higher than my login frequency suggests. It was more efficient for me to have everything offline where I could spread it out visually.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. My anxiety was through the roof, I was missing classes left and right, and I felt like I was drowning in six courses worth of content. There were weeks where I couldn't even bring myself to open the LMS because seeing how behind I was made my stress worse. The peer support intervention actually helped pull me out of that spiral—having people check in on me made a difference.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, I'd avoid the LMS entirely because it represented all this work I wasn't doing. Confusion made me procrastinate too—if I didn't understand something, I'd put off engaging with it rather than seeking help right away. Having extracurriculars and six courses meant something always had to give, and often it was consistent LMS engagement.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd be logged in, scrolling through materials, but my brain was completely elsewhere. I'd have videos playing but not really processing anything because I was too anxious about everything else I needed to do. Sometimes I'd click through content just to feel like I was doing something, but it wasn't real engagement. Those sessions were basically just going through the motions.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially when my anxiety was high. If an assignment felt overwhelming or I didn't understand the material well, I'd avoid it until the pressure of the deadline forced action. It's this weird cycle where knowing something is important actually makes the avoidance worse because the stakes feel higher. My resilience eventually kicked in, but not before a lot of procrastination.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to engage consistently. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly I'm logging in multiple times, watching videos, frantically completing tasks. My LMS activity probably looks like a flatline punctuated by spikes of intense activity right before due dates. It's not healthy, but it's how I survived this semester.
Q17: When I got good feedback on assignments, it motivated me to engage more with that course. My assignment scores were pretty high, so that positive reinforcement helped. But if feedback was delayed or vague, I'd lose momentum and drift away from the material. The peer support also provided informal feedback that kept me going during rough patches.
Q18: The structure honestly made things harder for me. Having six online courses meant constant competing demands, and the way content was organized in the LMS wasn't always intuitive for a visual learner like me. When modules were clearly laid out with video content front and center, I engaged more. When it was walls of text and buried resources, I checked out.
Q19: Video content and visual materials made engagement easier—that's just how my brain works. What made it harder was when important information was buried in text-heavy pages or when navigation was confusing. The discussion requirements felt forced and didn't match my learning style at all. Clearer visual organization and more multimedia content would have helped me stay engaged.
Q20: I think that could be someone who's trying to engage but struggling to actually complete work—maybe dealing with anxiety or confusion like I was. They might be logging in with good intentions but getting overwhelmed once they're there. Or they could be like me sometimes, clicking through things without really absorbing them, trying to feel productive without actually being productive.
Q21: That's literally me. My exam score and assignment scores were decent despite my low LMS engagement because I was doing tons of work offline. Visual learners like me often need to process information away from screens, making notes and diagrams by hand. The LMS activity doesn't capture the hours I spent with printed materials or studying with peers. Low login frequency doesn't equal low effort.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—like when you're active, how often you log in, what you click on. But they completely miss the quality of engagement and all the offline learning. They can't tell if someone is anxiously scrolling or deeply focused. They miss peer study sessions, handwritten notes, downloaded materials being reviewed, and the emotional state behind the numbers.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm making connections, creating visual representations of concepts, discussing ideas with peers, and applying what I'm learning. It's not about how many times I log in or how many minutes I spend on a platform. Real engagement happens in my head and in my notes, not necessarily on a screen.
Q24: The LMS can't show my growth through this semester—how I went from barely functioning to actually feeling okay by the end, thanks to peer support. It can't capture the anxiety attacks before logging in or the relief when I finally submitted something. It misses all my offline visual learning, my peer study sessions, and the resilience it took to pass despite everything. The data shows a struggling student, but it doesn't show someone who fought hard to make it through.
